data.title: REG - Wizz Air Holdings - Appointment of New Corporate Broker
data.description:
RNS Number : 7186H Wizz Air Holdings PLC 07 May 2025  

Appointment of New Corporate Broker

Ticker:  WIZZ

Geneva, 7 May 2025: Wizz Air Holdings Plc ("Wizz Air"), the fastest growing and most environmentally sustainable airline in Europe, announces that it has appointed Morgan Stanley & Co. International Plc as joint corporate broker, in conjunction with J.P. Morgan Cazenove.

- Ends -

About Wizz Air

Wizz Air operates a fleet of 231 Airbus A320 and A321 aircraft. A team of dedicated aviation professionals delivers superior service and very low fares, making Wizz Air the preferred choice of 63.4 million passengers in FY2025. Wizz Air is listed on the London Stock Exchange under the ticker WIZZ. The company was named one of the world's top ten safest airlines by airlineratings.com, the world's only safety and product rating agency, and named Airline of the Year by Air Transport Awards in 2019 and in 2023. Wizz Air has also been recognised as the "Most Sustainable Low-Cost Airline" between 2021-2024 and "Best Airline for Carbon Reduction" by World Finance Sustainability Awards in 2024. Wizz Air also received "EMEA's Environmental Sustainability Airline Group of the Year" by the CAPA-Centre for Aviation Awards for Excellence 2024.

For more information:

Investors:  Mark Simpson, Wizz Air                                                          investor.relations@wizzair.com

                 Betti Szanto, Wizz Air            

Media:       Andras Rado, Wizz Air                                                            communications@wizzair.com

                 James McFarlane/Eleni Menikou/Charles Hirst, MHP Group         wizz@mhpgroup.com

data.title: You Have a Message From Taiwan
data.description:
By Spencer Jakab

This is an online version of our Markets A.M. newsletter by Spencer Jakab. Get investing insights in your inbox each weekday by signing up here-it's free.

What do you think of Taiwan? Do you at all? Maybe it's time to start.

With about the same population as Florida, and a fourth of its land area, the island democracy is a small and faraway place. Americans mostly know it for two things-as a possible military flashpoint between the U.S. and China, which considers it a rogue province, and as the dominant force in semiconductors.

But something else entirely raised alarms this week. The Taiwanese dollar had a seemingly nuclear melt-up, rising so quickly and unexpectedly against the U.S. dollar that some strategists described it as a more than one-in-a-trillion event.

Maybe. But it isn't hard to trace this and many other recent, unusual moves to the trade war. The flip side of the deficits President Trump seeks to erase is that trading partners have recycled much of their earnings in dollars into U.S. investments.

Taiwan punches way above its weight in that respect, and its huge insurers mostly left themselves exposed to currency moves. With the greenback weakening in recent months, and the Taiwanese currency then surging, that has hurt. A Taiwanese life insurer that bought a boring 10-year Treasury note in September had lost as much as 17% on paper in its own currency at one point this week.

In the past week or so, something close to panic selling of dollars apparently set in among Taiwan-based investors. Other holders of U.S. investments, such as in Japan, will view it as a "warning shot," according to George Saravelos, who runs foreign-exchange research at Deutsche Bank. He warns of "self-fufiling moves."

U.S. Treasury debt, in particular, is thought to be so safe that default isn't even seen as a one-in-100 trillion possibility by foreign investors. America borrows in dollars and has a printing press, after all. But the trade war naturally focuses attention on the elephant in the room-what the real value of those dollars will be in the future.

That matters as much to ordinary American investors and consumers as it does to an Asian bank or insurer. U.S. interest rates are the bedrock of every investment on the planet and have underpinned a mostly golden era for American investors and consumers.

Would it be possible to keep interest rates on things like car loans and mortgages reasonable if foreigners decided one day that unsustainable federal debt and unpredictable policies made the U.S. a risky place to invest-sort of like an emerging market?

Yes, but it might require forceful steps from the Fed that stoke inflation, weakening the dollar even more. Regardless, Americans' savings could take on a decidedly emerging-market flavor, too. This would likely lead to a big drop in stock valuations and the sort of lifestyle they would finance.

That's a nightmare scenario, not a prediction. But the horror show that unfolded in markets halfway around the world this week should remind Americans what they have to lose.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at close
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex ended 0.1% higher to 80,746.78, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 index also advanced 0.1% to 24,414.4, paring losses seen at open, as ongoing tensions between India and Pakistan offset optimism from easing global trade concerns.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee dropped 0.5% to 84.8250 versus the U.S. dollar to logging its worst single-day decline in a month, as worries over an escalation in geopolitical conflict between India and Pakistan hit sentiment alongside a fall in Asian peers.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 103.17 rupees, with the yield down more than 1 bps at 6.3367% as buying resumed due to easing concerns around the Indo-Pak tensions and the overall bullish sentiment amid comfortable liquidity levels.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was down 3 basis points at 5.6%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) dropped 6 bps to 5.56%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5.50% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 5.73%

data.title: Chicago soy, corn and wheat higher on China-US trade optimism
data.description:
Chicago soybean and grain futures rose on Wednesday as news of a planned meeting between senior U.S. and Chinese officials boosted hopes for easing trade tensions between the world's two largest economies.

A weaker dollar and steady crude oil prices also helped underpin the grain markets, while investors awaited further direction from a U.S. Federal Reserve policy decision later on Wednesday.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) gained 1.1% to $10.53 a bushel, recovering from a 2-1/2 week low on Tuesday.

The soybean market has been particularly sensitive to the Sino-U.S. tariff battle, given China dominates global soybean imports, including a large portion of shipments from the United States, the world's second-largest exporter of the oilseed.

"Markets are higher across the board this morning, led by soybeans, following news that Chinese and U.S. officials will meet this week," CM Navigator analyst Donatas Jankauskas said in a note.

The planned meeting, which comes after the recent absence of clear steps towards de-escalating the tariff stand-off, also supported corn and wheat prices that have been pressured by improving crop weather in the United States.

CBOT corn rose 1.5% to $4.62-1/2 a bushel to move further away from a one-month low touched on Tuesday. CBOT wheat added 1.3% to $5.43 a bushel.

The wheat market has also drawn support from dryness concerns in Henan, a major wheat-producing province in China. Reduced output there could spur more Chinese demand for wheat imports.

data.title: Raw sugar prices fall on ample supplies, weak demand
data.description:
Raw sugar futures on ICE were lower on Wednesday with a strong start to the cane harvest in Centre-South Brazil ensuring supplies remain ample.

SUGAR

* Raw sugar ​fell 1.7% at 17.14 cents per lb at 1104 GMT, slipping back towards a more than three-year low of 16.97 cents set earlier this month.

* Dealers said there was little buying interest with speculators maintaining a net short position.

* White sugar fell 1.7% to $485.30 a metric ton.

COCOA

* New York cocoa ​​fell 0.1% to $8,914 a ton.

* Dealers said above-average rainfall in Ivory Coast last week should improve the size of the mid-crop although there remain significant concerns about the quality of the beans.

* They noted the outlook for 2025/26 crops was favourable in several countries including Nigeria, Cameroon, Ecuador and Brazil with high prices encouraging an expansion in production.

* London cocoa rose 0.4% to 6,480 pounds per ton.

COFFEE

* Arabica coffee fell 0.3% to $3.8880 per lb as the focus remained on the harvest in top grower Brazil.

* Brazil's 2025 coffee output is expected to total 55.7 million 60-kg bags, national food supply agency Conab said on Tuesday, a 7.5% increase from its January estimate.

* Arabica production was seen falling 6.6%, year-on-year, to 37 million bags and conilon (robusta) output rising 16.7% to a record 18.7 million bags.

* Robusta coffee rose 0.6% to $5,286 a ton.

data.title: Premium Brands Holdings Backs 2025 Rev C$7.20B-C$7.40B >PBH.T
JLL announces senior leadership appointments
data.description:
JLL announces senior leadership appointments

PR Newswire

CHICAGO, May 7, 2025

Karen Brennan appointed Chief Executive Officer of Leasing Advisory business globally

Kelly Howe to serve as next Chief Financial Officer of JLL

Appointments effective July 1, 2025

CHICAGO, May 7, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — JLL today announced the following senior leadership appointments, effective July 1, 2025.

Karen Brennan, who has served for the past five years as the company's Chief Financial Officer and sits on JLL's Global Executive Board, has been appointed as Chief Executive Officer of JLL's Leasing Advisory business globally.

Christian Ulbrich, President and CEO of JLL, said, "Throughout Karen's more than 25 year tenure at JLL she has taken on numerous leadership roles across the business globally, and exemplified strategic vision, excellence in execution and dedication to our clients. This appointment recognizes the contributions Karen has made to JLL's success, and our confidence in her ability to drive continued value for the stakeholders we serve."

Kelly Howe, currently Chief Financial Officer for JLL's Leasing Advisory business, will succeed Brennan as Chief Financial Officer. Howe joined JLL in January 2024 with more than 23 years of experience in professional services at Boston Consulting Group, most recently as their North America CFO. Upon becoming CFO, she will join JLL's Global Executive Board.

Ulbrich continued, "Since joining JLL, Kelly has distinguished herself through her combination of strategic insights and focus on execution, rapidly identifying key business drivers and developing enhanced analytics for decision making. We look forward to the great impact she will have as CFO and a member of our senior leadership team."

Andy Poppink, who currently serves as CEO, Leasing Advisory, will assume the role of CEO, Leasing Advisory, EMEA and APAC, reporting to Karen Brennan based out of Europe and step down from JLL's Global Executive Board.

Ulbrich concluded, "Andy is a tremendous leader whose deep relationships with clients and colleagues have had a meaningful impact on our company and our industry, and I look forward to continuing to work closely with him, Karen and the Leasing Advisory team."

About Karen Brennan

Karen Brennan has served as Chief Financial Officer of JLL since 2020. As a member of the Global Executive Board, she shapes and delivers JLL's strategic vision and transformation agenda.

Prior to becoming CFO, Karen spent 21 years in JLL's real estate investment management subsidiary, most recently as CEO of LaSalle Europe with overall responsibility for LaSalle's $22 billion AUM pan-European private equity real estate investment management business.

During the course of her more than 25 years of experience, Karen has lived and worked in the U.S., the U.K., Singapore and Hong Kong, with responsibilities across North America, Europe and Asia Pacific. She was previously a member of the LaSalle Investment Committees in North America, the U.K. and Europe.

Karen holds a Bachelor of Business Administration degree, Summa Cum Laude from Loyola University Maryland and an MBA from The University of Chicago Booth School of Business.

About Kelly Howe

Kelly Howe serves as Chief Financial Officer of JLL's Leasing Advisory business. Reporting to CFO Karen Brennan, she partners with global and regional leadership to drive performance, strategic initiatives and long-term financial growth, supporting both investor and occupier clients in achieving their real estate objectives.

Prior to joining JLL in 2024, Kelly built over two decades of experience in the professional services sector with Boston Consulting Group (BCG). At BCG, she held progressively more responsible roles, most notably serving as the first North America Chief Financial Officer. There, she spearheaded financial initiatives that fueled business growth, managed risk and optimized operational effectiveness across the region. Her work with BCG's Global and North America Finance teams encompassed a broad range of areas including business strategy, commercial acceleration and post-merger integration.

Kelly has an MBA from the Kellogg School of Management at Northwestern University and a Bachelor of Arts in Business Administration from the University of Washington.

Contact: Jesse Tron

Phone: +1 212 376 1215

Email: jesse.tron@jll.com

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/jll-announces-senior-leadership-appointments-302448524.html

SOURCE JLL-IR

data.title: Chipotle Mexican Price Target Maintained With a $49.00/Share by Stephens & Co.
Indian bond yields dip as border conflict jitters ease, sentiment holds strong
data.description:
Indian government bond yields closed a tad lower on Wednesday, as buying resumed due to easing concerns around the Indo-Pak tensions and the overall bullish sentiment amid comfortable liquidity levels.

The benchmark 10-year yield (IN067934G=CC) ended at 6.3367%, compared with its previous close of 6.3508%.

Investors believe the India-Pakistan conflict is unlikely to escalate further and could wind down soon, but will still closely watch the news for any retaliation from Pakistan, traders said.

Since Islamist militants attacked Hindu tourists, killing 26, in Indian Kashmir on April 22, the market has been trading cautiously, with the 10-year yield holding between 6.32% and 6.40%.

Traders expected the yield to jump to 6.40% on the day after India attacked Pakistan, but strong buying interest prevented that. Bond yields move inversely to prices.

"Bonds (prices) recovered during the day because the market viewed India's strikes in Pakistan as a limited event," said Gopal Tripathi, head of treasury and capital markets at Jana Small Finance Bank.

"In the absence of other negative indicators, whenever prices are going down, people are lapping up bonds."

Meanwhile, the banking liquidity surplus was at 1.37 trillion rupees as of May 6 and is expected to increase further as the central bank conducts three more bond purchases worth 750 billion rupees this month and announces its dividend payout.

The RBI's dividend to the government for the fiscal year is expected to be between 2.6 trillion rupees and 3 trillion rupees, according to IDFC FIRST Bank, more than market expectations.

RATES

India's overnight index swap (OIS) rates dipped, with traders pointing to a sustained receiving bias amid ample banking system liquidity.

The one-year (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) OIS rate fell 3 basis points to 5.60%, while the two-year (INR2YMIBROIS=CC) rate dropped 5 bps to 5.45% and the most liquid five-year (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) rate fell 6 bps at 5.56%.

data.title: Performance Food Fiscal Q3 Adjusted Earnings Fall, Sales Rise; Fiscal 2025 Revenue Outlook Revised
data.description:
Performance Food Group reported fiscal Q3 adjusted earnings Wednesday of $0.79 per diluted share, down from $0.80 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $0.87.

Net sales for the quarter ended March 29 were $15.31 billion compared with $13.86 billion a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $15.32 billion.

For fiscal 2025, the company said it now expects net sales of $63 billion to $63.5 billion, compared with its prior range of $63 billion to $64 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect $62.95 billion.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1131 GMT - Sterling could weaken if the Bank of England's policy decision on Thursday hints at ramping up interest rate cuts, TD Securities analysts say in a note. The BOE is likely to vote 8-1 to cut rates by 25 basis points, but with policymaker Swati Dhingra voting in favor of a 50 bp cut, they say. The BOE is also likely to downgrade its economic growth forecasts and drop the word "gradual" from its guidance for further rate cuts amid heightened trade uncertainty. "Inflation has come in below market expectations and monetary policy report projections, giving members confidence in continuing on a cutting path." Sterling is last down 0.1% at $1.3351. The euro rises 0.1% to 0.8517. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1114 GMT - Lenders Barclays and Standard Chartered were urged by a group of shareholders to set financing targets for the renewable energy sector at their AGMs this week. The group of investors together sit on hundreds of billions of pounds in assets under management and are coordinated by ShareAction, a nonprofit focused on responsible investing. While banks have taken positive steps in setting broad sustainable finance targets, they aren't yet providing a clear strategy on how to specifically fund the sectors and technologies at the forefront of the energy transition, campaign manager Kelly Shields says. "This is why investors are calling for Barclays to set a funding target for the renewable energy sector, and for Standard Chartered to publish a plan on how it will finance more renewable power in markets in the Global South," she says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0946 GMT - Orsted's decision to discontinue development of the Hornsea 4 offshore wind farm in the U.K. is an eye opener, Barclays analysts say in a note. The renewable-energy company continues to struggle, they say. Orsted will be hurt by the worsening news surrounding offshore wind in the U.S., uncertainty over tariffs for U.S. onshore wind, and the saturation of renewables in European markets. A turnaround would require clarity on U.S. tariffs and individual retirement savings plans, and evidence of investor appetite for secondary asset sales, Barclays says. The proceeds from these sales could be reinvested into higher-yielding areas. The added benefit of this would be reduced capex if assets are sold earlier in the construction cycle, Barclays adds.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0934 GMT - China's new stimulus and coming trade talks suggest that policymakers see greater urgency to support growth given the macro headwinds, says Richard Tang, China strategist and head of research for Hong Kong at Julius Baer. The moves are supportive of investor sentiment and puts a higher floor under the stock market, he writes in a note. The announcements also add positive momentum to equities, though more catalysts are needed to push stock indexes to their previous highs, Tang adds. Julius Baer reiterates its strategy of focusing on dividend stocks and domestic demand, especially consumption stocks. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - Socioeconomic factors amplify the exposure and vulnerability of the housing sector to extreme weather events as these become more common, according to a new report the Geneva Association, an insurance think tank. Addressing this requires an "all-of-society" approach with multiple stakeholders to implement measures such as risk prevention and resilience, the report reads. Incorporating physical climate risk into financial systems is also necessary to boost economic resilience, it says, pointing to incentives in insurance pricing, property valuation and mortgage lending.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0823 GMT - Most European asset managers still prioritize growth over acquisitions despite recent high-profile deals that might create an impression of accelerated consolidation, Morningstar says in a research report. Firms prefer to bet on expanding their products offerings, capabilities and operational efficiency, especially as competitive pressure grows, particularly from scale-driven U.S. firms, analyst Monika Calay writes. She notes that while selective consolidation--in the form of smaller bolt-ons rather than transformative transactions--may continue, the challenges often outweigh the benefits due to intense pricing pressures and integration hurdles. "Our research shows that [M&A] hasn't consistently delivered significant improvements in investment performance or cost savings for investors," Calay notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Sage group will unlikely upgrade its full-year guidance at upcoming interim results, Shore Capital analyst Martin O'Sullivan writes in a note. The accounting-software specialist's prospects remain promising and resilient, he says. Organic total revenue growth will likely stay in the 9% to 10% range during fiscal 2025 and 2026, he adds. However, "rising above this level would require stellar sales execution in terms of new customer acquisition and upsells," he says. Shares are down 0.4% at 1,248.50 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - Scor's quarterly result should reassure investors that management is on the path to delivering on the targets it has set out, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. "This could have positive implications on the group's cost of equity in the near term," analyst Darius Satkauskas writes. The French reinsurer posted a comfortable net profit beat, with a better undiscounted combined ratio and insurance service result. Shares, which have done well in the past month, rise 5% to 27.9 euros, taking its year-to-date gains to 18%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0811 GMT - BMW's confirmation of its full-year guidance came as a surprise and will be welcomed by investors, but more clarity is still needed, UBS analysts say in a research note. The German luxury-car maker said goals it set in March are still valid, even if the consensus among analysts was that new tariffs announced since then would have a bigger impact, UBS says. "BMW [investor relations] said the confirmed guide factors in some mitigation via pricing and also relaxation of some tariff measures later in the year;  neither was quantified and leaves some confusion at the moment," UBS says. In light of this, even a higher rate of annual buybacks might be on track, UBS says. Shares rise 3.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - Novo Nordisk's guidance cut is slightly greater than expected, with sales expectations lowered 3 percentage points and leaving consensus above the top end of the range, Jefferies analysts write. The EBIT target was also cut 3 percentage points. The lowered outlooks for sales and profit are likely 1-2 percentage points greater than expected, though potential consensus downgrades are likely greater due to currency. Sales of Wegovy missed by 7%, whilst Ozempic beat by 3%. Lower research and development and sales and distribution costs drove a 4% EBIT and 5% EPS beat. Focus now shifts to Wegovy trends and additional data readouts at the American Diabetes Association conference in June, Jefferies says. Shares rise 4.8%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0808 GMT - Novo Nordisk's downgraded guidance assumes a meaningful uplift of Wegovy volumes in the second half of the year and analysts at Morgan Stanley think the top end of the new guidance is still demanding. The guidance cut implies a 1% cut to consensus sales and EBIT at constant exchange rates but a 4% cut on sales and 6% cut on EBIT including currencies, the bank says. First quarter Wegovy sales missed expectations by 7%, impacted by destocking in the U.S. and compounding competition. Novo Novo also noted lower realized prices. Ozempic beat by 3%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0804 GMT - Scor reported an excellent start to the year, with meaningful consensus beats, Jefferies says in a research note after the French reinsurer posted its first-quarter results. "The result is all the more impressive when set against the Californian wildfires and management's proactive buffer building," analyst Philip Kett writes. The lack of positive or negative experience variances--the difference between the actual outcome of insurance claims and the insurer's predictions--in its life and health reinsurance division is reassuring after last year's hiccups and implies that Scor's actuarial estimates are now set more accurately, he adds. Shares rise 5% to 27.9 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Disney Stock Soars After Earnings. Why Theme Parks Are Impressing Investors.
data.description:
By George Glover

Disney stock was soaring early Wednesday after the theme-park and entertainment company reported quarterly earnings that beat Wall Street's expectations.

This is breaking news. Check back for more analysis soon.
